context: Turn over to 2 Chronicles if you will. And look at chapter 16.
point_summary: Faithfulness to God does not exempt believers from adversity, as illustrated by the prophet Hanani's confrontation with King Asa over his misplaced reliance on Syria rather than the Lord.

context: For the eyes of the Lord run to and fro throughout the whole earth to show Himself strong in the behalf of them whose heart is perfect toward Him.
point_summary: God's eyes continuously search the earth to strengthen those whose hearts are fully devoted to Him — a principle Asa forfeited by trusting Syria instead of the Lord, resulting in judgment rather than deliverance.

reference_text: chapter 24 of the book of 2 Chronicles
context: Skip over, if you will, and look at chapter 24. Chapter 24 of the book of 2 Chronicles, in my opinion, is a very sad passage of Scripture, a very unfortunate situation here with this one Joash, who was the king, and Joash had a very godly influence in his life.
point_summary: 2 Chronicles 24 illustrates that Joash's obedience depended entirely on Jehoiada's influence rather than a personal walk with God, warning that borrowed faith cannot substitute for one's own relationship with Christ.

context: ...which stood above the people and said unto them, Thus saith God, why transgress ye the commandments of the Lord that ye cannot prosper? Because ye have forsaken the Lord, He hath also forsaken you...
point_summary: God's faithfulness in sending messengers is met with violent rejection, as Zechariah—son of the very man who shaped Joash—was stoned at the king's command for delivering an unwelcome word of rebuke.

context: ...they conspired against him, Zechariah, and stoned him with stones at the commandment of... who? The king. Joash. ...in the court of the house of the Lord.
point_summary: Zechariah's murder by stoning—ordered by Joash himself, the very king Zechariah's father had shaped—illustrates that faithfulness to deliver God's message can cost the messenger his life, even at the hands of those closest to him.

context: Look in the book of 2 Chronicles, if you will, and look at Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, Jehoshaphat. I want you to look in 2 Chronicles chapter 17.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's example illustrates the second purpose of learning—dissemination of truth—as his heart lifted up in God's ways moved him to spread that truth throughout Judah.

context: in verse number six of 2 Chronicles 17. It says, and his heart was lifted up in the ways of the Lord.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's heart being "lifted up in the ways of the Lord" illustrates that personal devotion to God's ways naturally overflows into the dissemination of truth to others.

context: Notice it says in verse number seven, also in the third year of his reign, he sent to his princes, and it says to Ben-Hel and to Obadiah and to Zechariah and to Nethaniel and to Micaiah.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's commissioning of teachers to instruct throughout Judah's cities illustrates that learning truth carries the responsibility of disseminating it to others.

context: if my people which are called by my name shall humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways, so on and so forth
point_summary: Claiming God's promises in prayer—illustrated by how Israel's kings returned to 2 Chronicles 7:14—is presented as the proper response when God's people face difficulty.

context: The Bible tells us in verse number 5 that he sought God in the days of Zechariah. It tells us at the end of verse number 5 that as long as he sought the Lord, God made him to prosper.
point_summary: Uzziah's prosperity is traced directly to his seeking God, establishing the principle that sustained blessing flows from sustained seeking—and that prosperity itself becomes the danger point where self-sufficiency replaces dependence.

context: If my people which are called by my name shall humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways Then will I hear from heaven and will forgive their sin and will heal their land.
point_summary: God's conditional promise of revival in 2 Chronicles 7:14 is set alongside the judgment warnings of verses 19–22 to demonstrate that God's justice falls impartially even upon his own covenant people Israel.

context: But if you turn away and forsake my statutes and my commandments Which I have set before you and shall go and serve other gods and worship them
point_summary: God's judgment falls impartially on His own covenant people Israel for forsaking His statutes, demonstrating that divine justice is no respecter of persons, even toward those bearing His name.

context: the Lord God of their father sent to them by his messengers, rising up at times and sending because he had had compassion on his people and on his dwelling place
point_summary: God's persistent mercy in sending messengers is shown to have limits: when Israel mocked and despised those prophets, divine patience gave way to judgment with no remedy remaining.

context: They mocked the messengers of God and despised his words and misused his prophets until the wrath of the Lord rose against his people
point_summary: God's patience with Israel—expressed through repeated prophetic warnings sent in compassion—was not inexhaustible; persistent rejection of those messengers brought a point of no remedy where divine wrath could no longer be stayed.

context: The Bible says that he sought God in the days of Zechariah, who had understanding in the visions of God, and as long as he sought the Lord, God made him to prosper.
point_summary: Uzziah's early prosperity is traced directly to his seeking God, illustrating that divine blessing follows humble dependence on the Lord—a pattern soon shattered by pride.

context: In verse 7, the verse begins by saying, and God helped him.
point_summary: God's help to Uzziah in verse 7 is set as the foundation of his prosperity, making his later pride all the more tragic by contrast.

context: In verse number 8, at the end of the verse, it says that he strengthened himself exceedingly, and God was working, and God was allowing Uzziah to prosper.
point_summary: Uzziah's exceedingly strengthened position, granted by God's help, sets the stage for the pride that followed—illustrating how prosperity given by God can become the very occasion for forgetting its source.

context: and he made in Jerusalem engines, invented by cunning men, to be on the towers and upon the bulwarks, to shoot arrows and great stones withal. And notice what the Bible says, and his name was spread far abroad, for he was marvelously helped, and then notice these words, till he was strong.
point_summary: Uzziah's prosperity, divinely given, became the very occasion for pride — his heart lifted up "till he was strong" — illustrating how fleshly self-exaltation follows recognition and success.

context: And you look at verse 16, and it says, but when he was strong, his heart was lifted up to destruction.
point_summary: Uzziah's trajectory from God-given prosperity to pride-driven destruction illustrates how strength and recognition, if unchecked, lift the heart up and lead to ruin — a danger common to all flesh.

context: Nebuchadnezzar we spoke of in Uzziah's situation
point_summary: Uzziah's story in 2 Chronicles 26 is cited as a parallel to Nebuchadnezzar's: pride drove him to overstep his God-assigned role, and immediate divine judgment—leprosy—confirmed that pride leads to destruction.

context: And we find here in the text of Scripture in 2 Chronicles chapter number two that I am thankful to be able to say that Solomon responded properly. In verse number one, notice the Bible says, of 2 Chronicles chapter number two. and Solomon determined to build a house for the name of the Lord and a house for his kingdom.
point_summary: Solomon's determined response to build the house of the Lord illustrates that when God's will has been clearly communicated, the only fitting response is firm resolution, not half-hearted intention.

context: And Solomon told out three score and ten thousand men to bear burdens, and four score thousand to hew in the mountain, and three thousand and six hundred to oversee them.
point_summary: Solomon's determined response to God's revealed will — marshaling tens of thousands of workers — illustrates that true resolution is not half-hearted effort but settled, purposeful action in obedience to a known divine calling.

context: sent to Haram the king of Tyre, saying, as thou didst deal with David my father, and didst send him cedars to build a house to dwell therein, even so deal with me.
point_summary: Solomon's determined response to God's revealed will—immediately mobilizing resources and workers—is held up as a model of resolute, wholehearted obedience rather than tentative, half-hearted effort.

context: Behold, notice what he says in verse number four, I build a house to the name of the Lord my God, to dedicate it to him, and to burn before him sweet incense, and for the continual showbread, and for the burnt offerings, morning and evening, on the Sabbaths, and on the new moons, and on the solemn feast of the Lord our God. This is an ordinance forever to Israel.
point_summary: Solomon's determined resolve to build a house dedicated to God's name—marked by worship, offerings, and perpetual ordinance—is presented as the model response when God's revealed purpose is clearly understood.

context: I believe that we see in verses one through four of 2nd Chronicles 2 that Solomon is responding with determination. I find that in verses five and six that he responds with humility and reverence.
point_summary: Solomon's determination to build is tempered by humility, as his declaration that "the house which I build is great" is grounded not in self-confidence but in the surpassing greatness of God above all gods.

context: I believe that we see in verses one through four of 2nd Chronicles 2 that Solomon is responding with determination. I find that in verses five and six that he responds with humility and reverence.
point_summary: Even the grandest earthly temple cannot contain God, a reality Solomon confesses in humility, recognizing that the greatness of the task demands reverence toward the One no house can hold.

context: and the house which I build is great. Why? For great is our God above all gods.
point_summary: Solomon's declaration that the house he builds is great *because* God is great frames the entire building project as an act of humility and reverence before One too vast for any structure to contain.

context: but who is able to build him in house? Seeing the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain him.
point_summary: Solomon's rhetorical question — "who am I that I should build him a house, seeing the heaven of heavens cannot contain him?" — frames temple service not as human achievement but as humble privilege granted by a God too great to be housed.

context: Notice in verse 7, send me now therefore a man cunning to work in gold and in silver and brass and iron and purple, crimson, blue, and that can skill to grave with the cunning men that are with me in Judah and in Jerusalem, whom David my father did provide.
point_summary: Solomon's response to the call to build demonstrates understanding — he knew what resources were needed and moved with determination to secure skilled craftsmen and materials for God's house.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

point_summary: 2 Chronicles 3:1 marks the decisive transition from all prior planning and preparation to actual commencement, illustrating that God's charge to "arise and be doing" demands action, not endless planning.

context: And he began to build in the second day of the second month in the fourth year of his reign.
point_summary: The date recorded in 2 Chronicles 3:2 marks the moment preparation gave way to action — the point where planning ended and the actual work of building the house of the Lord commenced.

context: we think about his response and we look at these portions of Scripture in 2 Chronicles 2 and 2 Chronicles 3
point_summary: Second Chronicles 2 represents a stage of continued preparation and gathering of materials and workers that preceded the actual commencement of building, illustrating that embracing an assignment moves from planning into engaged action.

context: we think about his response and we look at these portions of Scripture in 2 Chronicles 2 and 2 Chronicles 3
point_summary: 2 Chronicles 3:1–2 marks the decisive transition from preparation to action, as Solomon's commencement of building illustrates that embracing God's assignment must move beyond planning into actual engagement with the work.

context: In chapter number 5 of 2 Chronicles, the Bible says in verse number 1, thus, all the work that Solomon made for the house of the Lord was, what's it say? Isn't that good? It was finished.
point_summary: Solomon's completion of the temple—"the work was finished"—is held up as the direct result of embracing and engaging the assignment David charged him to do.

context: It came even to pass as The trumpeters and singers were as one to make one sound to be heard in praising and thanking the Lord
point_summary: When completed work is offered in unified praise, God responds — the "then" of divine glory filling the house depends entirely on the faithful doing that precedes it.

context: Then the house was filled with a cloud even the house of the Lord So that the priests could not stand to minister by reason of the cloud for the glory of the Lord had filled the house of God
point_summary: The word "then" in verse 13 signals that God's glory filling the temple was contingent on obedience — Solomon had to arise and do the work before he could witness that divine response.

context: So much so that the Bible tells us that the priest could not stand to minister by reason of the cloud
point_summary: The glory of the Lord filling the temple so completely that priests could not stand to minister is presented as the direct consequence of Solomon's obedience in completing the work God assigned him.

context: now when Solomon had made an end of praying... the fire came down from heaven and consumed the burnt offering and the sacrifices and the glory of the Lord filled the house
point_summary: God's visible glory filling the completed temple in 2 Chronicles 7:1 is presented as the direct consequence of Solomon's obedience to the charge given him — a "then" that only comes after faithful doing.

context: the priests could not enter into the house of the Lord because the glory of the Lord had filled the Lord's house
point_summary: God's glory filling the house so completely that priests could not stand to minister was the direct result of Solomon's obedience to the charge given him — no obedience, no glory.

context: when all the Children of Israel saw how the fire came down the glory of the Lord upon the house They bowed themselves with their faces to the ground upon the pavement and worshiped and praised the Lord saying for he is good
point_summary: The visible glory filling the temple—witnessed by all Israel—came only because Solomon obeyed the charge to arise and build; obedience to God's assignment is the precondition for seeing God work.

context: verse 11 of 2nd Chronicles 7 says thus Solomon finished the house of the Lord and the king's house and All that came into Solomon's heart to make in the house of the Lord and in his own house. He prosperously affected
point_summary: Solomon's faithful completion of all that was in his heart to build stands as the sermon's proof that obedience to God's charge results in God visibly moving and the people worshiping.

Howbeit, he entered not into the temple of the Lord and the people did yet corruptly.
point_summary: Jotham's personal righteousness—doing right before the Lord and avoiding his father Uzziah's sin—did not prevent the people under him from continuing in corruption, setting the stage for his wicked son Ahaz.

context: Ahaz was 20 years old when he began to reign, and he reigned 16 years in Jerusalem.
point_summary: Despite a godly heritage through grandfather Uzziah and father Jotham, Ahaz personally chose to reject the Lord and walk in the idolatrous ways of Israel's kings.

context: he walked in the ways of the kings of Israel. What are some of the things that this man Ahaz did? In v. 2 it says that he had idols.
point_summary: Ahaz's descent into idolatry—making molten images, burning incense, and sacrificing his own children in the fire—illustrates the catastrophic endpoint of following ungodly influences down sin's slippery slope.

context: it tells us in v. 3, moreover he burnt incense in the valley of the son of Hinnom and burnt his children in the fire.
point_summary: Ahaz's idolatry escalated to child sacrifice by fire in the valley of Hinnom, illustrating how ungodly influences drive sin down a steepening slope toward the most extreme abominations.

context: You find here at the end of verse number 23 in 2 Chronicles 28 it says,
point_summary: Sin's deceitful and destructive power is illustrated by Ahaz, whose idolatry became "the ruin of him and of all Israel" — a pattern the sermon applies to desensitization and pornography's cascading consequences.

point_summary: Congregational worship, as seen in Hezekiah's revival, unites singing and instruments not for human recognition but as genuine praise directed wholly to God.

context: We find that here in 2 Chronicles, there's a great revival during Hezekiah's time.

point_summary: Contemplation and self-examination, as drawn from 2 Chronicles 7, will humble believers by exposing self-deception and reminding them they are sinners saved by grace.

context: That reference that we made in 2 Chronicles 7 in verse number 14, it says at the end of those items that are listed after humble themselves, pray, seek My face, it says, turn from their wicked ways.
point_summary: Genuine confession — turning from wicked ways as 2 Chronicles 7:14 commands — follows honest self-examination and cannot be bypassed by mistaking God's long-suffering patience for His approval of sin.

context: See, the layout of 2 Chronicles 7 and verse number 14, if my people which are called by my name shall humble themselves and pray and seek my face.

context: The Lord said to Solomon, I have heard thy prayer.
point_summary: God's attentiveness to Solomon's prayer in 2 Chronicles 7 is cited as scriptural precedent for the assurance that God hears His people when they pray.

context: and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways, then will I hear from heaven and heal their land. That's what the second Chronicle 7 in verse number 14.
point_summary: 2 Chronicles 7:14 is cited as a textbook example of a conditional "if-then" promise, contrasted with the unconditional Abrahamic covenant to establish that not all divine promises share the same structure.

context: In the book of 2 Chronicles, in chapter number 26, the Bible tells us about Uzziah.
point_summary: Uzziah's leprosy illustrates that the priestly office cannot be self-assumed—even a king whom God blessed faced divine judgment for crossing the boundary between royal and priestly roles.

context: but I want you to notice what the Bible says in 2 Chronicles 36 and verse 21.
point_summary: Israel's 70-year Babylonian captivity is presented as the direct fulfillment of Leviticus 26's warning, the land receiving the Sabbath rests that generations of disobedience had withheld from it.

context: In 2 Chronicles 36 and verse number 21, the Bible says to fulfill the word of the Lord by the mouth of Jeremiah until the land had enjoyed her Sabbaths. For as long as she lay desolate, she kept Sabbath to fulfill threescore and 10 years.
point_summary: The 70-year Babylonian captivity fulfilled Leviticus 26:33's warning that the land would receive its Sabbaths by force what Israel had refused to give it in obedience.

context: Go back to 2 Chronicles 26, if you will. 2 Chronicles 26 gives us the record of King Uzziah.
point_summary: King Uzziah's story is introduced as an Old Testament illustration that pride is destructive, with his characterization as a basically good king who sought and was helped by the Lord establishing the contrast to come.

context: and he, speaking of Uzziah, did that which was right in the sight of the Lord.
point_summary: Uzziah's record of doing right before the Lord and seeking God establishes the baseline of his character before pride enters and becomes his undoing.

context: and he, Uzziah, sought God in the days of Zechariah, who had understanding in the visions of God, and as long as he sought the Lord, God made him to prosper.
point_summary: Uzziah's prosperity under God's blessing—rooted in seeking the Lord—becomes the setup for understanding how strength, when it arrives, can become the very occasion for pride's destructive turn.

context: and God helped him.
point_summary: God's help and blessing upon Uzziah is presented as the foundation of his strength, making the subsequent turn toward pride all the more tragic and instructive.

context: for he strengthened himself exceedingly. Moreover, Uzziah built towers in Jerusalem...
point_summary: Uzziah's growing strength, enabled by God's help, set the stage for the pride that ultimately led to his destruction when his heart was lifted up.

context: for he was marvelously helped, till he was strong.
point_summary: Uzziah's story illustrates that divine blessing and strength, described as being "marvelously helped," became the very occasion for pride that lifted his heart up to his destruction.

context: but when he was strong, his heart was lifted up to his destruction.
point_summary: Uzziah's strength, built through God's marvelous help, became the very occasion for pride — his heart lifted up to his own destruction the moment he ceased to recognize the source of his prosperity.

context: His heart was lifted up, and the Bible says, to His destruction. For He transgressed against the Lord his God. He went into the temple of the Lord to burn incense upon the altar of incense.
point_summary: Uzziah's strength, given by God's help, became the very occasion of his pride—his heart "lifted up" drove him to transgress priestly boundaries, and God judged him with leprosy.

context: And we find that there were some men that withstood him, and according to v. 17, it says that they were valiant men and they withstood Uzziah the king.
point_summary: Azariah and the priests who confronted Uzziah are identified as valiant men who withstood the king's prideful transgression of entering the temple to perform a role reserved exclusively for priests.

context: And we find that he's wroth with them in v. 19,
point_summary: Pride's destructive trajectory is illustrated in Uzziah's leprosy breaking out mid-defiance—God's immediate judgment striking him in the very act of his presumptuous, anger-fueled transgression.

context: Look over, if you will, in the book of 2 Chronicles in chapter 34. In 2 Chronicles chapter 34, God speaks to us about one of the kings of Judah, and really, Josiah here in this passage of Scripture, what a great man of God,
point_summary: Josiah's revival under his leadership in Judah is introduced as a biblical illustration of what a tender, prepared heart toward God can produce in a nation.

context: We find that it's in this portion of Scripture in 2 Chronicles 34 that God gives us some insight to the man Josiah.
point_summary: Josiah's revival under young leadership illustrates that God can use youth as catalysts for national spiritual renewal, even amid widespread wickedness and idolatry.

context: And the Bible tells us in verse number 14 of 2 Chronicles 34 that he had given the instruction with regard to the work and to repair the house of the Lord and it says that when they brought out the money that was brought into the house of the Lord, Hilkiah the priest found a book of the law of the Lord given by Moses.
point_summary: While Josiah's workmen were repairing the neglected temple, Hilkiah the priest discovered the book of the Law of Moses—a recovery of God's Word that became the catalyst for national revival under a young king.

context: now in the first year of Cyrus, king of Persia, that the word of the Lord spoken by the mouth of Jeremiah might be accomplished.
point_summary: God's sovereignty over human rulers is demonstrated in His stirring the spirit of Cyrus, a pagan king, to fulfill Jeremiah's prophecy and authorize the rebuilding of the temple after 70 years of captivity.

context: He actually made a couple of really bad decisions in 2nd Chronicles chapter number 20
point_summary: In the face of overwhelming conflict and chaos, God's sovereignty is the anchor—illustrated through Jehoshaphat's crisis in 2 Chronicles 20, where a good king confronted a great military threat beyond his own power to resolve.

context: it came to pass after this also that the children of Moab and the children of Ammon and with them other Beside the Ammonites came against Jehoshaphat to battle
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's sudden military crisis in 2 Chronicles 20:1 establishes the context of overwhelming difficulty against which his declaration of dependence on God in verse 12 is set.

context: Then there came some that told Jehoshaphat saying there cometh a great multitude Against thee from beyond the sea on this side Syria and behold They be in hazes on Tamar which is in getting
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's crisis — a vast enemy coalition advancing against him — establishes the sermon's central situation: a good man facing overwhelming odds with no human solution in sight.

context: Oh our God Will thou not judge them? For we have no might against this great company that cometh against us Neither know we what to do But our eyes are upon thee
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's confession — "we have no might" and "neither know we what to do" — models the posture of utter dependence on God as the only right response to overwhelming circumstances.

reference_text: 2nd Chronicles chapter number 20 in verse number 3
context: But there are times in our lives as human beings that we fear It's an emotion that we have and there are times in which we fear in Jehoshaphat here in 2nd Chronicles chapter number 20 in verse number 3 when he's faced with this dilemma
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's fear in the face of an overwhelming threat did not paralyze him but drove him to seek God, establishing fear as a catalyst for earnest, corporate seeking of the Lord.

context: you get into 2 Chronicles. 6 and 7, and you read the prayer of Solomon
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's prayer in 2 Chronicles 20 draws directly on Solomon's temple dedication prayer in 2 Chronicles 6–7, claiming God's recorded promise to hear and help when his people cry to him in affliction.

context: God answers, I've heard thy prayer, is what God says to Solomon in chapter number 7
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's prayer draws directly on God's promise in 2 Chronicles 6–7—that when his people cry out in affliction before his house, he will hear and help—as the covenant basis for seeking divine aid.

context: Jehoshaphat, back in 2nd Chronicles chapter number 20, he knew that God had said this.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's decision to fast and seek God in 2 Chronicles 20 is grounded in his confidence that God had promised to hear and help those who seek Him.

context: Mattaniah, a Levite of the sons of Asaph, came the Spirit of the Lord in the midst of the congregation, and he said, Hearken ye, all Judah, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, and thou, King Jehoshaphat, thus saith the Lord unto you.
point_summary: The Spirit of the Lord coming upon Jehaziel in the assembly is presented as God's direct answer to Jehoshaphat's corporate prayer, confirming that God hears and responds to His people when they seek Him.

context: Remember what he said in verse number nine, that then that will hear and help, and God is answering the prayer.
point_summary: God's faithfulness to hear and answer prayer is confirmed as the Spirit of the Lord speaks directly in response to the corporate cry for help voiced in verse 9.

context: Notice what he says in verse 15, Be not afraid nor dismayed by reason of this great multitude, for the battle is not yours, but God's.
point_summary: God's answer to Jehoshaphat's corporate prayer is the assurance that the battle belongs not to His people but to Him, a declaration applied here to the spiritual warfare believers face today.

context: Ye shall not need to fight in this battle. Set yourselves, stand ye still, and see the salvation of the Lord with you. O Judah and Jerusalem, fear not, nor be dismayed. Tomorrow, go out against them, for the Lord will be with you.
point_summary: In spiritual warfare, God's people are called not to fight in their own strength but to stand still and witness his salvation, assured by his promised presence.

context: Jehoshaphat bowed his head with his face to the ground, and all Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem fell before the Lord, worshiping the Lord.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's immediate prostrate worship upon receiving God's promise illustrates that certainty in God's word produces rejoicing and adoration before the promised deliverance ever arrives.

context: And the Levites, and the children of the Kohathites, and of the children of the Korhites, stood up to praise the Lord God of Israel with a loud voice on high.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's people praised God before the battle was won because their certainty in God's promised word made pre-deliverance worship not only possible but fitting.

context: And they rose early in the morning, and went forth into the wilderness of Tekoa. And as they went forth, Jehoshaphat stood and said, hear me, O Judah, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem. Believe in the Lord your God, so shall you be established. Believe his prophets, so shall you prosper.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's appointment of singers to praise God before the battle illustrates that certainty in God's promised word produces rejoicing and worship even prior to the actual deliverance.

context: Jehoshaphat again stands up in verse 20, and he says, believe in the Lord your God.
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's call to believe God in verse 20 grounds the people's pre-deliverance rejoicing: their certainty that God would do what He said made praise possible before any evidence arrived.

context: In verse 21, they're there and they're praising the beauty of holiness, that they're praising the Lord and the beauty of his holiness, and it says, praise the Lord for his mercy endureth forever.
point_summary: Praising God "in the beauty of holiness" before the battle's outcome was decided expressed the people's certainty that God would do what He had promised, making their worship an act of faith rather than a response to evidence.

context: And notice verse 22, the beginning of the verse, it says, and when they began to sing and to praise, the Lord set ambushments against the children of Ammon Moab and Mount Seir.
point_summary: Praise offered in faith before any visible deliverance moved God to act, because Judah's rejoicing preceded the evidence of what he had promised to do.

context: this great passage in 2 Chronicles 20, this event in the life of Jehoshaphat
point_summary: Jehoshaphat's confession "our eyes are upon thee" models the posture of dependent faith in which God alone receives glory precisely because the believer admits he cannot figure out his own dilemma.

context: Brother Hummel preached a message from 2 Chronicles 7 and verse number 14 in dealing with revival.
point_summary: 2 Chronicles 7:14 is cited as a revival text preached at a pastor's meeting, connecting the broader call to corporate prayer with the sermon's ongoing theme that God's people need a renewed prayer life.

context: ...look on Friday evening at that truth in 2 Chronicles 7 and verse number 14...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God responds to Solomon and says, I have heard thy prayer
point_summary: God's responsiveness to prayer is confirmed across Scripture, with Solomon's temple dedication cited as a clear biblical example of God hearing and answering when his people cry out to him.

context: we find that as we look throughout the Word of God, I often think of this particular example in 2nd Chronicles 24. If you want to turn there just for a moment and look at this passage and we can see this over in Kings as well, but here in 2nd Chronicles chapter number 24 and I just want to draw your attention very quickly to a couple of points within this passage of Scripture
point_summary: Joash's faithfulness lasting only as long as Jehoiada lived illustrates why inherited or mentored faith must become one's own personal relationship with God.

context: 2nd Chronicles 24 and verse 15 for the Bible says that Jehoiada waxed old and was full of days when he died.
point_summary: Jehoiada's death exposed that Joash had no personal walk with God, illustrating why every believer must develop an individual relationship with God independent of any human mentor or influencer.

context: Notice in verse 17 it says, Now after the death of Jehoiada came the princes of Judah and made obeisance to
point_summary: When the mentor Jehoiada died, Joash's collapse revealed he had no personal walk with God of his own — illustrating why every believer must develop an individual relationship with God, not one borrowed from another's influence.

context: yet he sent prophets, plural, to them. Now I like, listen, every word of God is pure. He didn't just send one prophet to them. He sent prophets to them. And it says to bring them again unto the Lord.
point_summary: God's persistent sending of multiple prophets to restore Israel demonstrates that rejected instruction grieves a gracious God who desires repentance, not judgment.

context: as I read those verses of Scripture from 2 Chronicles and from the Psalms where God says He sent His prophets
point_summary: God's repeated sending of prophets, as referenced from 2 Chronicles, reveals that His driving purpose is restoration — He sends correction because He wants His people to return.

context: Second Chronicles chapter 28 this morning. And we of course just finished our missions conference...
point_summary: God uses individual believers—however ordinary they may feel—to make a difference for His glory, as illustrated by the account in 2 Chronicles 28.

context: And as I was experiencing the conference, hearing the preaching from God's word and being challenged in my own life...
point_summary: God uses individual believers—however ordinary they may feel—to make a difference for His glory, as illustrated by the account in 2 Chronicles 28.

context: I was encouraged and the Lord just kept bringing me back to this portion of scripture in Second Chronicles chapter number 28...
point_summary: God uses individual believers to make a difference for His glory, countering the notion that one person is too insignificant to matter, as illustrated through the account in 2 Chronicles 28.

context: as we look at this portion of scripture in 2 Chronicles chapter number 28, the portion of scripture that we're going to examine provides with us a glimpse into the life of a prophet by the name of Oded.
point_summary: God uses individual believers — even relatively obscure ones like Oded, whose name means "restorer" — as channels of His honor and glory, a pattern traceable across biblical, church, and modern history.

reference_text: Ahas was 20 years old when he began to reign, and he reigned 16 years in Jerusalem
context: In verse number one, the Bible says, Ahas was 20 years old when he began to reign, and he reigned 16 years in Jerusalem, but he did not that which was right in the sight of the Lord, like David his father.
point_summary: Ahaz's failure to walk in righteousness, explicitly contrasted with David, established the moral ground for God's judgment upon Judah through the surrounding nations.

reference_text: he made molten images for Balaam
context: For he walked in the ways of the kings of Israel and made also, notice the first indictment against him in verse number two, he made molten images for Balaam.
point_summary: Ahaz's manufacture of molten images for Baal stands as the first and defining indictment of his reign, illustrating Judah's persistent sin of idolatry in direct violation of God's command against graven images.

context: And burnt his children in the fire, after the abominations of the heathen, whom the Lord had cast out before the children of Israel.
point_summary: The word "moreover" in verse 3 introduces a cumulative indictment against Ahaz, illustrating what the sermon calls the slippery slope of sin—each transgression compounding into greater abomination.

context: He sacrificed also and burnt incense in the high places and on the hills and under every green tree.
point_summary: Ahaz's escalating sins — from idolatry to child sacrifice to worship on every high place — illustrate what the sermon calls "the slippery slope of sin," the dangerous, deceitful progression that results from forsaking God.

context: And notice verse number 9. But a prophet of the Lord was there. But a prophet of the Lord was there whose name was Oded.
point_summary: Into a scene of military triumph, fanfare, and unchecked wickedness, God's prophet appears—standing alone against the momentum of an entire victorious nation.

context: As best we can tell from that portion of Scripture in 2 Chronicles 28, Oded did not benefit one thing by standing up and declaring the message of God.
point_summary: Oded's courageous obedience in 2 Chronicles 28 demonstrates that one individual, gaining nothing personally, can be used by God to directly impact 200,000 lives.

context: Turn over if you will to 2 Chronicles chapter number 32. In 2 Chronicles chapter 32, we find that in this portion of Scripture...
point_summary: When enemies seek to discourage and weaken God's people, Hezekiah's charge to "be strong and courageous" illustrates how a leader can support the weak by countering fear with faith.

context: with him is an arm of flesh, but with us is the Lord our God to help us
point_summary: Hezekiah's declaration that God—not an arm of flesh—fights for His people illustrates that supporting the weak means pointing them to God as their ever-present helper.

context: If my people, which are called by my name, shall humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways, then will I hear from heaven and will forgive their sin and heal their land.
point_summary: Seeking God is presented as the indispensable condition for revival, with 2 Chronicles 7:14 anchoring a chain of biblical texts showing that God's people must actively seek His face or forfeit salvation's blessings.

context: Chronicles chapter 24 this evening, 2 Chronicles chapter number 24
point_summary: Scripture's record of Joash's life is presented as profitable and divinely preserved instruction, from which practical lessons are drawn especially for young people entering a new season of life.

context: but Jehoiada waxed old and was full of days when he died. He was 130 years old, and that's when he died
point_summary: Jehoiada's death at 130 marks a decisive turning point in Joash's life, illustrating that a spiritual mentor will eventually pass into eternity, making a personal walk with God indispensable.

context: now after the death of Jehoiada, and we're gonna notice in just a moment what takes place
point_summary: Verse 24:17 marks a devastating turning point in Joash's life, illustrating that a personal walk with God cannot be borrowed from a mentor—when Jehoiada died, Joash had nothing of his own to sustain him.

context: The Bible tells us in verse 18 that they left the house of the Lord God of their fathers and served groves and idols, and wrath came upon Judah and Jerusalem for this their trespass.
point_summary: Abandoning the house of God they had once repaired, Judah's leaders followed flattering princes into idolatry, demonstrating how quickly godly influence removed can reverse even sincere-seeming devotion.

context: Zechariah, the son of Jehoiada, the priest, came and stood and delivered a message and the Bible says that they conspired to kill him
point_summary: When Joash allowed the princes of Judah to influence him, the resulting spiritual decline led him to stone Zechariah—the son of the very priest who had protected him—for proclaiming truth.

context: thus Joash the king remembered not the kindness which Jehoiada, his father, had done to him but slew his son
point_summary: Joash's murder of Zechariah—despite Jehoiada's lifelong kindness—illustrates how corrupting influence accelerates sin until even intimate bonds of loyalty are destroyed.

context: the Bible says in verse number 19 that he, God, sent mess, prophets to them to bring them again unto the Lord, and they testified against them, but they would not give ear.
point_summary: God's faithfulness in sending prophets to warn His people stands as a call to maintain a tender, receptive heart toward His word, lest hardness lead to the tragic outcome of "they would not give ear."

context: And the Spirit of God came upon Zechariah, the son of Jehoiada, the priest, which stood above the people and said unto them, thus saith God, why transgress ye the commandments of the Lord, that ye cannot prosper? Because ye have forsaken the Lord, he hath also forsaken you.
point_summary: Forsaking the Lord by refusing to give ear to His word brings an inability to prosper, making sensitivity to Scripture essential to remaining in God's blessing.

context: In verse number six, Jehoiada is still alive, and the Bible tells us this in verse number six. It says in the king, that's Joash, he called for Jehoiada the chief. And notice what he says to him, why hast thou not required the Levites to bring in out of Judah and out of Jerusalem, the collection.
point_summary: Joash's appeal to "the commandment of Moses" in verse 6 shows that obedience to the written Word of God was the stated basis for the temple collection, contrasting with the later hardness of verse 19.

context: And yet we find that in verse number 19, as the prophets of God stand before Him and testify
point_summary: God's prophets testifying against Joash and being refused a hearing illustrates the danger of growing calloused to God's Word after once walking in obedience to it.

context: The Bible says, and it came to pass at the end of the year that the host of Syria came up against him, and they came to Judah and Jerusalem and destroyed all the princes of the people from among the people, and sent all the spoil of them unto the king of Damascus.
point_summary: God's long-suffering and mercy must never be mistaken for approval of disobedience, as Judah's defeat by a small Syrian army demonstrates that He ultimately executes judgment on those who forsake Him.

context: For the army of the Syrians came with a small company of men, and the Lord delivered a very great host into their hand, because they had forsaken the Lord God of their fathers. So they executed judgment against Joash.
point_summary: God's judgment can fall through a numerically inferior force, demonstrating that military outcome is determined by divine will, not human strength — just as with Gideon.

context: when they departed from him, for they left him in great diseases, his own servants conspired against him for the blood of the sons of Jehoiada, the priest, and slew him on his bed, and he died. They buried him in the city of David, but they buried him not in the sepulchres of the kings.
point_summary: Disobedience culminating in apostasy brought divine judgment on Joash, whose own servants conspired to assassinate him—and he was denied burial among the kings as a final mark of disgrace.

context: And just before, or just at the end of the text of Scripture that records for us the Babylonian captivity of Judah...
point_summary: The seventy-year Babylonian captivity is shown to be the land forcibly receiving the Sabbath rests Israel had refused to observe, directly fulfilling Jeremiah's prophecy through their disobedience.

reference_text: if my people which are called by my name will humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways
context: if my people which are called by my name will humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways. Those four prerequisites must be met by God's people.
point_summary: Revival comes only when God's people meet all four prerequisites of 2 Chronicles 7:14—humbling, praying, seeking, and turning—with none omitted.

context: will humble themselves and pray and seek my face and turn from their wicked ways, then will I hear from heaven, then will I forgive their sin, and then will I heal their land.
point_summary: Genuine national and personal renewal hinges on God's people humbling themselves, praying, seeking God's face, and turning from sin—conditions the sermon applies directly to individual believers today.

context: I want you to look over the Old Testament, look of 2 Chronicles, if you will, and I want you to look at this example, and I'm gonna tell you it's a negative example, but I believe that we learn from this example in 2 Chronicles chapter 33.
point_summary: Manasseh's reign in 2 Chronicles 33 is introduced as a negative example illustrating how the lives others observe carry far greater weight than the words they hear.

context: Manasseh was 12 years old when he began to reign, and he reigned 50 and five years in Jerusalem.
point_summary: Manasseh's reign beginning at age twelve introduces a negative example of how a father's compromised legacy shapes the next generation, illustrating that someone is always watching.

context: Wherefore, the Lord brought upon them the captains of the host of the king of Assyria, which took Manasseh among the thorns and bound him with fetters and carried him to Babylon.
point_summary: God's judgment on Manasseh through Assyrian captivity is presented as the consequence of refusing to hearken to God's voice, a fate the sermon urges believers to avoid by humbling themselves before affliction rather than after.

context: And when he was in affliction, notice this in verse 12, he besought the Lord his God and humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers.
point_summary: Though God mercifully heard Manasseh's prayer of humility in affliction, the sermon presses that it is far better to humble oneself and heed God's voice before judgment comes.

context: that we take heed to the voice of God, to the word of God, that we hearken to his voice as they should have in verse number 10.
point_summary: God's warning to Manasseh went unheeded, illustrating that hearing God's word and truly receiving it are two distinct responses with serious consequences.

context: nevertheless, in verse 17, the people did sacrifice still in the high places yet unto the Lord their God only
point_summary: Even after Manasseh's genuine repentance and decree that Judah serve God, the people continued sacrificing at the high places, illustrating that righteousness cannot be legislated or forced—it is a matter of the will and heart.

context: In verse 20, it says, so Manasseh slept with his fathers. They buried him in his own house and Amon, his son, reigned in his stead
point_summary: Manasseh's death and succession in verse 20 serves as a narrative hinge, pivoting attention to son Amon, whose evil life demonstrates that a parent's repentance does not guarantee a child's righteousness.

context: It tells us in verse 21 that he was two and 20 years old when he began to reign and reigned two years in Jerusalem
point_summary: Amon's reign is introduced here only to contrast his unrepentant evil with his father Manasseh's late-life return to God, illustrating that a parent's restoration does not guarantee a child will follow the same path.

context: In verse 22, it says, but he did that which was evil in the sight of the Lord
point_summary: Amon's replication of Manasseh's earlier idolatry—not his repentance—illustrates that a parent's return to God does not guarantee a child will follow, making the influence we model a sobering responsibility.

context: as you read the end of 2 Chronicles chapter 36, you begin to see how quickly some of those just reigned for a very, very short period of time
point_summary: The rapid succession of four kings following Josiah's death, as recorded at the end of 2 Chronicles 36, illustrates the swift moral and political collapse that formed the backdrop of Jeremiah's ministry on the eve of Babylonian captivity.

context: 2 Chronicles chapter number nine
point_summary: The Queen of Sheba's own words in 2 Chronicles 9:8 are cited to show that God's love for Israel was the stated reason He established Solomon as king over them.

context: blessed be the Lord thy God, which delighted in thee to set thee on his throne to be king for the Lord thy God
point_summary: The Queen of Sheba's declaration that God made Solomon king over Israel because He loved Israel illustrates divine love expressed through sovereign, purposeful action on behalf of His people.

context: the book of 2 Chronicles 5
point_summary: The same glory that filled Moses' tabernacle later filled Solomon's completed temple, establishing a pattern that obedient, finished work precedes the manifest presence of God.

context: And you find that in v. 4, the Bible says,
point_summary: Solomon's declaration in 2 Chronicles 2:4 — "I build a house to the name of the Lord my God" — is cited as part of the temple preparations that parallel Moses finishing the tabernacle work.

context: thus all the work that Solomon made for the house of the Lord, notice this phrase, was finished
point_summary: Just as Moses finished the work of the tabernacle, Solomon finished the work of the temple — the same language marking the completion of each dwelling place God ordained for His glory.

context: and the house which I build is great. Why? For great is our God above all gods
point_summary: Solomon's completion of the temple—"the work was finished"—is set in parallel with Moses finishing the tabernacle, showing a recurring biblical pattern of faithful servants completing God's appointed work.

context: even to prepare me timber and abundance for the house which I am about to build shall be wonderful great
point_summary: Solomon's declaration that the temple "shall be wonderful and great" (v. 9) illustrates the abundance of preparation required for a house built to honor a great God, paralleling the tabernacle's construction leading to Exodus 40.

context: it came even to pass as the trumpeters and singers were as one to make one sound to be heard in praising and thanking the Lord
point_summary: When unified worship — trumpeters and singers as one — preceded the glory of the Lord filling Solomon's completed temple, it mirrored the same divine pattern seen when Moses finished the tabernacle in Exodus 40.

context: the glory of these chapters in 2 Chronicles
point_summary: The glory filling Solomon's temple in 2 Chronicles mirrors Exodus 40, with both events sharing the pattern that divine presence followed obedience — the word *then* marking the moment blessing came after faithful compliance.

context: Can I remind you of a king of Israel by the name of Uzziah? Uzziah was a king
point_summary: Uzziah's presumption in entering the sanctuary illustrates that even a prosperous, God-blessed king could not perform priestly service, because that office belonged exclusively to those appointed to it.

context: we could look in 2 Chronicles as well
point_summary: Hezekiah's story is introduced as a parallel witness to Psalm 50:15, with 2 Chronicles noted as an alternate passage before the sermon settles in Isaiah 38.

context: for now they shall say we have no king because we feared not the Lord. What then should a king do to us? They have spoken words, swearing falsely in making a covenant
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in 2 Chronicles chapter 24, Joash goes to Jehoiada and says, hey, why aren't we having them bring the offering as the commandment of the Lord by the servant of the Lord Moses
point_summary: Giving is a biblical principle with deep Old Testament roots, illustrated by Joash's appeal to restore the Mosaic offering so God's provision could flow through God's people to repair His house.

context: 2 Chronicles chapter 16 is where we'll begin
point_summary: God's eyes search the whole earth to show himself strong on behalf of those whose hearts are perfect toward him, revealing the kind of heart—sensitive and submissive—in which God works.

context: we find this morning in 2 Chronicles chapter 16, notice what the Bible says in
point_summary: God's eyes search the whole earth to show himself strong on behalf of those whose hearts are perfect toward him, revealing the kind of heart—sensitive and submissive—in which God works.

context: Therein thou hast done foolishly, therefore from henceforth thou shalt have wars
point_summary: God actively searches the whole earth to show himself strong on behalf of those whose hearts are perfect toward him, contrasting the sensitive, submissive heart with Asa's hardened, resistant one.

context: then Asa was wroth with the seer
point_summary: Asa's wrathful rejection of Hanani's rebuke—imprisoning the prophet rather than repenting—illustrates the kind of hardened, unsubmissive heart in which God cannot work.

context: he was wroth with the seer, not only did he get angry with him, but he put him in prison
point_summary: When God's word through Hanani confronted Asa's unrepentant heart, Asa's wrathful response—imprisoning the prophet—illustrates that an unright heart will get mad at the preacher rather than get right with God.

context: look here if you will in chapter 17, 2 Chronicles 17, look in verse number 3, and the Lord was with Jehoshaphat
point_summary: God's presence with Jehoshaphat is tied directly to Jehoshaphat's character and conduct—his walking in David's ways and seeking not after idols.

context: he walked in the first ways of his father David and sought not unto Balaam
point_summary: God's presence with Jehoshaphat is traced directly to Jehoshaphat's obedience and character—walking in David's first ways and seeking the Lord rather than the practices of Israel.

context: he sought to the Lord God of his father and walked in his commandments and not after the doings of Israel
point_summary: God's presence with Jehoshaphat is traced directly to his obedient heart—seeking the Lord and walking in His commandments rather than following Israel's corrupt ways.

context: therefore the Lord established the kingdom in his hand and all Judah brought to Jehoshaphat presence and he had riches and honor and abundance
point_summary: God's establishment of Jehoshaphat's kingdom is presented as the direct result of a heart devoted to walking in the Lord's commandments, illustrating that divine blessing follows faithful obedience.

context: he was marvelously helped until he was strong in verse 16
point_summary: Uzziah's example in 2 Chronicles 26:15–16 serves as a cautionary contrast: divine help and strength became the occasion for pride, and that pride led to his destruction.

context: when he was strong, his heart was lifted up to his destruction. That's Uzziah in 2 Chronicles 26 verses 15 and 16
point_summary: Uzziah's strength became the occasion of his ruin—his heart lifted up in pride led to his destruction, contrasting the God-honoring heart-elevation seen in Jehoshaphat.

context: I want you to consider 2 Chronicles chapter 34
point_summary: God's work in the heart is illustrated by Josiah's example in 2 Chronicles 34 as the last genuine revival in Judah's history, offered as a contrast to hearts hardened, proud, or choked by sin.

context: Notice in verse number 7, the Bible says in 2 Chronicles 34 and verse 7, and we had broken down the altars and the groves and had beaten the graven image into powder and cut down all the idols throughout all the land of Israel
point_summary: Josiah's demolition of altars, idols, and groves throughout the land illustrates the outward fruit of a heart genuinely turned toward God, contrasted with the hardened or pride-filled heart that chokes out God's work.

context: he burnt the bones of the priests upon their altars and cleansed Judah and Jerusalem
point_summary: Josiah's burning of the priests' bones on their altars is cited as one concrete act of cleansing that characterized his broader campaign to purge idolatry from Judah and Jerusalem.

context: to repair the house of the Lord, his God
point_summary: Josiah's heart to repair the Lord's house, despite the temple's desecration through idolatry, sets the stage for the rediscovery of God's Word and the revival that followed.

context: when they brought out the money that was brought into the house of the Lord, Hilkiah the priest found a book of the law of the Lord given by Moses
point_summary: During Josiah's temple-cleansing reforms, the rediscovery of the Book of the Law by Hilkiah the priest marks the moment when God's Word was restored to its rightful place in Israel's life.

context: Hilkiah answered and said to Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the Lord
point_summary: The rediscovery of the Book of the Law during Josiah's temple repairs marks the moment God's Word was restored to its central place in Judah's life and worship.

context: it says in verse 17, and they have gathered together the money that was found in the house of the Lord and have delivered it into the hand of the overseers and the hand of the workmen
point_summary: The faithful stewardship of the temple's collected money—delivered to overseers and workmen—illustrates the ordered, accountable response that flows from a heart already being moved toward obedience and repair.

context: it came to pass in verse 19, when the king had heard the words of the law, that he rent his clothes
point_summary: Josiah's tearing of his clothes upon hearing the law illustrates that a heart made tender by God responds to His Word with immediate, visible brokenness and humility.

context: thine heart was tender and thou didst humble thyself before God when thou heardest his words against this place and against the inhabitants thereof and humblest thyself before me and didst rent thy clothes and weep before me, I have even heard thee also saith the Lord
point_summary: A tender heart toward God's word — demonstrated by Josiah's weeping, self-humbling, and torn clothes — is the condition God acknowledges and responds to with mercy.

context: the king sent and gathered together all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem and the king went up into the house of the Lord and all the men of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem and the priests and the Levites and all the people great and small
point_summary: Josiah's tender heart, moved by hearing God's word, compelled him to gather all the people so they too could hear the words of the covenant read aloud.

context: he read in their ears all the words of the book of the covenant
point_summary: Josiah's tender heart, moved by hearing God's Word, compelled him to gather all the people so they too could hear the words of the covenant read aloud.
